name: Rosemary Clooney
abstract: Rosemary Clooney (May 23, 1928 – June 29, 2002) was an American singer and actress. She came to prominence in the early 1950s with the song "Come On-a My House", which was followed by other pop numbers such as "Botch-a-Me", "Mambo Italiano", "Tenderly", "Half as Much", "Hey There", "This Ole House", and "Sway".

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Flanigan
about_quotes.evidence: Scott Hamilton and Tony Bennett and Rosemary Clooney and that kind of stuff.
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Flanigan interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 15, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Mays
about_quotes.evidence: I was very young, Rosemary Clooney’s pianist, Paul Moore, got sick and couldn’t make the gig in Australia. And I got the call... I’d never worked with a big band. And I hadn’t worked with that many singers.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bill Mays interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, December 5, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: David Finck
about_quotes.evidence: I played for Peggy Lee and many, many others, Rosemary Clooney. ... I remember playing for Rosemary Clooney toward the end.
about_quotes.doc_title: David Finck interviewed by Monk Rowe, Utica, New York, May 29, 2015

about_quotes.speaker: Jack Sheldon
about_quotes.evidence: I did a few concerts with Benny back then, and I got to go back with Rosemary Clooney, we did a tribute to Bing Crosby.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack Sheldon interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Bobby Militello
about_quotes.evidence: when I did Rosemary Clooney with Sam Noto... we did it at the Art Park... I was on tenor on that gig
about_quotes.doc_title: Bobby Militello interviewed by Monk Rowe, Buffalo, New York, October 14, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Laurence Juber
about_quotes.evidence: that record with Rosemary Clooney was really cool... playing with somebody of that kind of caliber
about_quotes.doc_title: Laurence Juber interviewed by Monk Rowe, Glasgow, Scotland, July 27, 2016 [interview 1 of 2]
